title: Deceit and Contention End in Anguish!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The letter incorporates extended quotations from contemporary reporting in *The Hindu* and *Junior Vikatan*. Those reports, their figures, and their attributions are kept distinct from Kalaignar's own argument. The Tamil reading copy below repairs only source-confirmed OCR defects; the canonical Tamil source remains unchanged.
paragraphs: During the past five years of her rule, instead of fulfilling the promises she made to the people and safeguarding the welfare of the country, Jayalalithaa has concentrated on a chain of exploits. For reasons known to everyone, she deliberately permits her ministers to engage continually in irregularities and corruption. When their conduct exceeds every limit and makes the public recoil, she stages the fraudulent drama of saying, “So many things happened without my knowledge,” and removes them from the Cabinet. Once the people forget everything, thinking, “Amma has taken action, after all,” she brings them back into the Cabinet. Through this cycle, she has turned Tamil Nadu into a hunting ground for selfish interests.

She is now throwing dust in the people's eyes and adopting another tactic. With or without her knowledge, certain ministers accumulated enormous amounts of money and property. She is now said to be using the police to intimidate and compel them, conducting a hunt to make them surrender those assets. Although most newspapers conceal this, a few, fearing their conscience, continue to publish the details.

As the AIADMK Government's five-year term draws to a close, let me cite just a few passages from a news analysis by Mr Samas in the Tamil edition of *The Hindu* about the crooked administration of this regime:

> “In a wonder unmatched by any other State in India, Jayalalithaa changed her Cabinet twenty-four times within five years. Of the ministers who took office with her in 2011, only one-third remain in office today. > > “Why has not even a First Information Report been registered against ministers who have committed major economic crimes? Why have these hardened criminal offenders not been arrested? Why has no action begun to subject them to a comprehensive and swift investigation and secure the punishment prescribed by law? If ‘all are equal before the law,’ are ministers exempt from it? Is it enough merely to summon them personally, conduct a secret inquiry and negotiation, and then sideline and isolate them instead of removing them immediately from the Cabinet? > > “When Jayalalithaa said in 2011 that the DMK Government had emptied the treasury before leaving office, the example she gave of the DMK's administrative failure was the debt of one lakh crore rupees it had left behind. When this Government's term ends in 2016, Jayalalithaa will leave behind a debt of Rs 2.11 lakh crore. The debt left by the DMK was at least a continuation of debts accumulated by successive governments over time. In its five-year term alone, the AIADMK Government has created an even larger debt. What kind of administration does this demonstrate? > > “In the School Education Department alone, ministers have been changed six times within five years. If a minister has no chance of remaining in a department for even a little more than a year on average, what condition can that department possibly be in?”
Thus did *The Hindu* publish these various details.
Similarly, an article in *Junior Vikatan* titled “5 Years... 50 Ministers: Ministers Tossed About 23 Times” said:

> “When the AIADMK Government assumed office in May 2011, a total of thirty-four people, including Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, took charge as ministers. On 27 June, Mohammed John was inducted into the Cabinet, and the portfolios of some ministers were also changed. Within days of the first Cabinet reshuffle, J. carried out the next one and made her ministers tremble. Following an allegation against Law Minister Isakki Subbiah, she removed him from the Cabinet on 3 July 2011. Senthur Pandian was inducted in his place, and within two months of the new Government taking office, a third Cabinet swearing-in ceremony was held. > > “The next shock came in November. On 4 November, the Tamil Nadu Cabinet underwent a whirlwind reshuffle. Six ministers were removed and six new ministers sworn in. The decline of Sengottaiyan, who had wielded influence in the Cabinet, also began with this reshuffle. His portfolio was changed. Minister Paranjothi, who became embroiled in controversy, resigned on 9 December 2011. Minister Selvi Ramajayam was also stripped of office. M.S.M. Anandan and Valarmathi entered the Cabinet in their place. Sengottaiyan, who had roamed about as a powerful minister, was thrown into the unimportant Information Technology Department, moved again to the Revenue Department, and then lost his ministerial post in the sixth reshuffle. > > “The department most affected by the Cabinet reshuffles was School Education. C.V. Shanmugam held that portfolio in the first Cabinet. Within a few months, he was replaced and the department entrusted to Agri Krishnamurthy, who had newly joined the Cabinet. A few months later, it moved from him to N.R. Sivapathi, then to Vaigaichelvan, and afterward became an additional portfolio for Higher Education Minister Palaniappan. It was tossed from one to another in this manner. Finally, the portfolio came to rest with Health Minister K.C. Veeramani. > > “Among those who took office as ministers with J. when she became Chief Minister, subsequently lost office, and then returned as ministers were Gokula Indira, R.B. Udhayakumar, B.V. Ramanaa, S.P. Shanmuganathan, S.P. Velumani, M.S.M. Anandan, Agri Krishnamurthy and N.R. Sivapathi. Of those granted a second opportunity in the Cabinet, Agri Krishnamurthy and N.R. Sivapathi were the two who lost it again. Most recently, two months ago, B.V. Ramanaa lost his post because of a picture circulated on WhatsApp. > > “Ordinarily, a Cabinet swearing-in ceremony takes place only once every five years. But in the past five years, the entire Cabinet was sworn in three times: first when the Cabinet initially took office; a second time under OPS in 2014 after J. lost her post; and a third time when J. returned as Chief Minister in 2015. Cabinet swearing-in ceremonies have taken place at Raj Bhavan more than fifteen times. That too is a record. > > “During the five years from 2011 to 2016, the Cabinet underwent twenty-three reshuffles. From the change that began with Mariam Pitchai to Chinnaiya, who was removed last month, nearly half a hundred MLAs have occupied ministerial chairs during these five years.”
That is how *Junior Vikatan* described the game of tossing ministers about under Jayalalithaa's rule.

Some newspapers have written not only about these ministerial changes but also about how serving ministers act contrary to the duties they are legally required to perform. Consider the stream of reports concerning senior ministers in Jayalalithaa's Government, their assistants, and the officials who support them. Here is one report from *The Hindu* article:

> “It is said that senior Cabinet ministers O. Panneerselvam, Natham Viswanathan, Palaniappan, Vaithilingam and Palanisamy collected money from more than a hundred people without the leadership's knowledge, promising to secure them places to contest the coming Assembly election. > > “It is said that O. Panneerselvam and Natham Viswanathan engaged in corruption and irregularities and accumulated enormous property. Reports say that they were summoned by the leadership and questioned, and that action is continuing to confiscate all the assets they accumulated improperly. After one minister was removed from office, a case was registered alleging that he had engaged in financial fraud. An assistant to another minister was arrested for collecting money from and cheating several people by promising to secure contracts in government-affiliated institutions. This is not merely an internal AIADMK affair. It concerns the Government, the interests of the State, and the people. The money the ministers are alleged to have obtained through irregularities is the people's money. The wrongs attributed to them are all economic crimes.”
*The Hindu* article stated this clearly.

There was also a report in the newspapers, made public by the owner of the bus itself, that a minister from Mannargudi had loaded sack after sack of money and jewellery onto an omnibus and brought them to Chennai.

Because Chief Minister Jayalalithaa and the AIADMK Government have so far offered no official response to allegations of corruption and daylight robbery, people believe that all the reports appearing in newspapers and statements are true. Is everything being concealed out of fear that, if action is taken against the ministers, they may reveal details of the tribute they paid to the higher leadership?

In statements responding to these extremely serious newspaper reports, political party leaders have said that, whether or not public-welfare work took place after the AIADMK assumed office in Tamil Nadu in 2011, corruption, irregularities and exploitation have certainly functioned full-time as the principal work of every department. Across the State, people are discussing the several thousand crores of rupees said to have been invested by one minister in Casa Grande, the company constructing an apartment complex called “Amarendra” on East Coast Road; an expensive hotel in New York City; and Oakley Property Services, a London real-estate company.

Some political leaders state that the money and property now taken from senior ministers alone are worth Rs 30,000 crore; that the proceeds have been credited to the account of the higher leadership; that an investigation must be conducted; and that money collected through irregular means must be deposited in the Government treasury. But what law permits money and property worth many thousands of crores, accumulated by ministers through abuse of power, irregularities and corruption, to be confiscated and credited to a private account?

Why has not even a First Information Report been registered against ministers who have committed major economic crimes? Why have these hardened criminal offenders not been arrested? Why has no action begun to subject them to a comprehensive and swift investigation and secure the punishment prescribed by law? If “all are equal before the law,” are ministers exempt from it? Is it enough merely to summon them personally, conduct a secret inquiry and negotiation, and then sideline and isolate them instead of removing them immediately from the Cabinet?

Chief Minister Jayalalithaa and the AIADMK Government have evaded their duty to answer these questions and doubts before the people of the country. Because they have so far offered no official response to allegations of corruption and daylight robbery, people believe that all the reports appearing in newspapers and statements are true. Is everything being concealed out of fear that, if action is taken against the ministers, they may reveal details of the tribute they paid to the higher leadership?

To split the votes that are multiplying and gathering day by day against the ruling AIADMK, she is deploying schemes and conspiracies by using the mountain of money she has amassed, along with certain journalists and intelligence personnel. Such deceit may bring a little pleasure, but “deceit and contention end in anguish!” Jayalalithaa cannot possibly escape the democratic punishment that the people of Tamil Nadu will deliver through the coming election!

To conceal every criminal act and divert attention, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has not summoned for interview everyone who paid money and submitted an application seeking to contest the election. Instead, merely for appearance's sake, she has invited only a few people to her own house and staged a drama that deceives the people of the country and even members of her own party. To split the votes that are multiplying and gathering day by day against the ruling AIADMK, she is deploying schemes and conspiracies by using the mountain of money she has amassed, along with certain journalists and intelligence personnel. Such deceit may bring a little pleasure, but “deceit and contention end in anguish!” Jayalalithaa cannot possibly escape the democratic punishment that the people of Tamil Nadu will deliver through the coming election!
With affection,
M.K.
